item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The images that were used in this article have been replaced with the following images, which are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Title: Couple of Schistosoma mansoni.jpg
Author: علاء
Release year: 2016

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: Hymenolepis microstoma.jpg
Author: Magdalena ZZ
Release year: 2011

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert changes at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

A website for connecting people to parasites? Well, as it turns out there are quite a few parasitic SCPs lurking around (SCP-940, SCP-1027, SCP-1429, SCP-2829, and SCP-3060 to name a few) and I don't see why it wouldn't make sense for each of them to have an entry on that site. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1624 is a website located at the IP address ██.██.███.███. The front page proclaims it as "www.loveinside.███." Layout and design of the website appears to be designed for online romantic encounters between parasitic organisms and their host. Profiles on the website encountered so far include approximately ███ different types of organisms.

To this:

SCP-1624 is a website located at the IP address ██.██.███.███. The front page proclaims it as "www.loveinside.███." Layout and design of the website appears to be designed for online romantic encounters between parasitic organisms and their host. Profiles on the website encountered so far include approximately ███ different types of organisms1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Entries for instances of SCP-940, SCP-1027, SCP-1429, SCP-2829, and SCP-3060 have been observed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has issues, stated above by Dewie Dewitt and wishun. Other than that, this is fantastic. It balances in the thin red line between a normal article and a -J. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have parasitophobia (fear of parasites) And this just made it fifty billion times worse…..
Take my upvote and get out of my face lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. It's funny, it's simple, it's comical. I'm glad this isn't a joke SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Godammit Roget.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…being open to infection, and being more prone to infection."
Aren't those the same thing? I have no medical knowledge to speak of, but they strike me as equivalent.

There is no mention of attempting to trace a physical location, which is a pretty obvious first step for containment. If no physical location exists and/or can't be found, that is a secondary anomaly that needs to be written in.

Similarly, interviewing the parasites would be a pretty obvious line of investigation, but it isn't mentioned. A chat log with a tapeworm trying to be charming could be both entertaining and revoltingly horrific.

Are the human members real people? Finding them would be a big priority because they are basically breaches in progress.

Final opinion: Not terrible, but I didn't find it engaging. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wording of some sentences could be changed to reflect a more official SCP report: 'The subjects may say that they are able to communicate with the instance' to 'Subjects report being able to communicate with the instance,' and 'as time progresses, the subject may begin to form a romantic relationship with the SCP-1624-1 instance' to 'as time progresses, subjects begin to form a romantic relationship with the SCP-1624-1 instance' so the read comes across that the writing is factual instead of hypothesised. Didn't want to edit just in case I am 100% wrong in that format.

A really enjoyable read, giving into a creepy-crawly horror was a treat. Though unfortunently not an actual SCP, I think it's just too fun to not keep somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the difference here would be that there is no actual creature, and the presence of a real creature actually changes the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now it's just a little too Blackwood. "Small creature that communicates and acts like people" is a little overplayed. Sorry, but I don't think I'll be sold on this particular concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I meant to change that to parasitic years. I've adjusted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't upvote anything that treats "tapeworm years" like it's a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'subjects may form a romantic relationship with the thing supposedly living in their stomach' bit is a lovely dollop of horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dare you to do it. With a "Lovely Roulette" and everything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the the picture of the b-ball worm at the end is the absolute limit of what you can fit into an article without making it a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love that idea. It does sound like a joke, but in a sense it's not more ridiculous then Factory Porn: some computer stuff that messes up people's life.

The medical aspect could be a bit more precise: the 1st paragraph of description suggests any internal parasites are available, but later paragraphs refer to tapeworms only. Also, you mention stomach at some point, but the tapeworms lives in the intestine. There are all kinds of parasitic worms that live in different organs, so I suggest the description could be either more specific or more general.

Not to mention the deadly fuzz-worm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :D No problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now personally, I rather like it. I've had the thought of an anomalous Internet account for a while now, and the parasite's profile made me laugh. It might fit more as a -J, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for helping me fix it

you da best

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I feel that it needs more than what's already there. It's a little too bare-bones for me.

No vote.

EDIT: Is better, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clever and well written, but seems more of a parody than actual SCP.
EDIT: Rewrite feels more substantial. Upvote from me.

item.title: Re: Additions
item.content:encoded: Better here and there, but not enough to change my vote. There's still not much here to work with, I'm sorry.

item.title: Re: Additions
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: Re: Additions
item.content:encoded: Still not feeling it. Sorry, man

item.title: Additions
item.content:encoded: I've added to it to make it more obviously anomalous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fithin'

Seriously, with all the bizarre dating sites out there, you would have to wonder why the foundation would take this one in particular seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote. Like some of the others, I feel this has crossed the line between "silly SCP" and a plain old -J. Plus, it isn't explicitly anomalous, which I can kind of overlook if the article's entertaining enough, but this just isn't for me as it is. I do feel this could work if the idea and execution were tweaked in the right direction, though.

Edit: Definitely better, but I'm still not totally digging it. I guess the article in general just isn't for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fourth. And this isn't actually that funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, and I am therefore not downvoting.

But I'm also not upvoting, because the dissenters are right. There is absolutely nothing here to indicate that the Foundation is not containing a joke website for no reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, while I think this is a hilarious joke it doesn't really fit into the whole SCP schtick. It really needs something to hook(worm) in that it really is the parasites posting profiles and not some practical joker. Maybe have a worm post something SCP classified in their profile due to currently infesting an agent? (Currently seeking new attachment as current roomie is on double-keter duty guarding a large lizard, and I don't want to be caught in the cross fire ?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding. Because I read this three times over trying to find the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could literally make this website right now, even with my very limited website creation abilities, and even make it so that you would probably find each parasite mentioned on the webpage at the locations you are directed to. There's just no way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding. Nothing in this indicates that it's anomalous unless we can prove it's not just an internet prank.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jumping on the bandwagon against. Also, even if it were anomalous enough to warrant containment, I'm not sure why it's Euclid; we seem to have contained it with little or no effort whatsoever.

On an unrelated formatting note, I'm not sure why the addenda are centered. Everyone else has pointed out all of my other complaints.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reads too much like a -J for me. Also, like others have said, it doesn't feel particularly anomalous. Just weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's certainly original, but it's not very interesting. Like RhettSarlin said, this could easily just be a joke site for people who like to swallow tapeworms or something. I'm not saying that it's bad; I just think it's boring.

item.title: I'm in agreement.
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing about this that needs to be contained. It's like taking The Onion seriously.

Also, what's with the redaction for the IP address and the URL? You aren't redacting enough of it to be worthwhile, and a value above 255 is invalid, in any case. Looks rather like a Comcast home networking address, aside from that. (I used to work for Comcast in their repair department) Looking at your hostmask in the chat, I'd say it was derived from your own.

Also, if you wanted to shut down an IP within the US, just toss a DMCA request at their IP address, and use ICANN to block off the name server.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really seeing it here. Not really enough details to show why this thing is anomalous enough to warrant the Foundation's attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way too lulzy for me, I'm sorry. It's funny but I can't in good conscience upvote this.

*edit* actually, no. Hold the hell up. How is this even provably ANOMALOUS? Why would the Foundation even care about it? Someone could easily set up a website like this as a JOKE. How would anybody be able to tell the difference between it being a joke and being legitimate? Because you can go to such and such location and ingest a parasite? Come on.

This is less of an SCP than even a -J article, and I am disappointed in you for posting this. You're better than this. It'll get upvoted I've no doubt, for reasons similar to why ___-J does, but that doesn't make it good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an original concept, but it's way too short for me, and overall, it comes across as trying too hard to be clever. Also, not too big a fan of parasite articles. Put some more meat on the bones and I might change my mind, but for now, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What the f-NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO NO" - Site Director Rogers, upon discovery of SCP-1624

Anywho, this was awesome to read. Short, sweet and fun. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original, awesome concept. Good fun to read and overall well written.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good lord, I don't even know what to think of this other than that it deserves an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I never thought it would be possible to make parasites amusing. Good job, Roget.

After careful consideration, I have to agree with Rhett. Sorry, man. While it was amusing, it wasn't really anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Credit to salvagebar and Jukse for riffing with me in chat, and giving me some of the bits for the profile

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, it's very unique. There's not much I could say about it except that I thoroughly enjoyed it.

+1
